skill.name: npm-git-install
skill.description: Install npm packages directly from GitHub repositories using git URLs. Use when installing packages from private repos, specific branches, or unreleased versions not yet on npm registry.
skill.summary: Documentation guide for installing npm packages from GitHub repositories using HTTPS, SSH, or token authentication.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: This skill is documentation-only (markdown guide). Static analyzer flagged 119 patterns in code examples, but all are FALSE POSITIVES. The skill teaches legitimate npm/git workflows. Minor risk: documents sudo usage and credential handling patterns, but explicitly recommends secure practices (env vars over hardcoded tokens, SSH over passwords).

medium_findings.title: Documentation References sudo Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents sudo usage for fixing npm permissions (line 196). While this is legitimate system administration, users could misuse sudo. The skill does recommend safer alternatives (~/.npm-global).
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: sudo commands present in documentation examples, but skill explicitly recommends non-sudo alternatives as preferred approach

low_findings.title: Documents Credential Handling Patterns
low_findings.description: The skill documents GitHub token usage and SSH key setup. While it recommends secure practices (env vars, SSH), providing these patterns could be misused if users don't follow the security guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Credential patterns documented but skill explicitly recommends secure handling via environment variables and SSH keys

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives - Command Patterns in Documentation
low_findings.description: 61 'external_commands' detections are markdown code examples, not executable skill code. Pattern matches like $(whoami) and backticks are bash examples in documentation context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All detected patterns are in markdown code blocks - this is documentation teaching users commands, not a skill that executes code

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives - URL Patterns
low_findings.description: 23 'network' detections are documentation URLs (github.com, npm docs, stackoverflow) and git URL examples. These are reference links, not network calls made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs are documentation references and git URL syntax examples, not actual network requests

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives - Filesystem Paths
low_findings.description: 24 'filesystem' detections include /tmp paths, ~/.ssh, ~/.npmrc references. These are documentation examples showing where npm/git store files, not actual file operations by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File paths appear in documentation examples explaining npm/git behavior, not skill file operations

content.user_title: Install npm Packages from GitHub Repositories
content.value_statement: When packages are not on npm registry or you need specific branches, this skill guides you through installing directly from GitHub. Learn HTTPS, SSH, and token authentication methods safely.
actual_capabilities: Install npm packages using git+https URLs with branch, tag, or commit references
Configure SSH key authentication for private repository access
Set up GitHub personal access tokens for npm authentication
Troubleshoot common installation errors (permissions, authentication, build failures)
Manage globally installed packages from GitHub sources
limitations: Requires git to be installed on the system before use
Cannot execute installation commands - provides documentation guidance only
Requires network access to GitHub for package installation
Private repositories require valid SSH keys or personal access tokens

use_cases.title: Install Unreleased Package Versions
use_cases.description: Install packages from specific branches or commits before they are published to npm registry
use_cases.target_user: Developers testing beta features or bug fixes

use_cases.title: Private Organization Packages
use_cases.description: Install internal packages from private GitHub repositories using SSH or token authentication

use_cases.title: Fork Package Installation
use_cases.description: Install modified fork versions of packages with custom patches or features

prompt_templates.title: Basic Git URL Installation
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me install an npm package from GitHub using the repository URL
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner - First time installing from GitHub

prompt_templates.title: Specific Branch Installation
prompt_templates.prompt: Install the package from the develop branch using git URL syntax with branch reference
prompt_templates.scenario: Installing pre-release versions from development branches

prompt_templates.title: Private Repository Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through setting up SSH key authentication for installing packages from private GitHub repositories

prompt_templates.title: Token Authentication Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure npm to use GitHub personal access tokens for authentication. Show me how to set up .npmrc and environment variables securely
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced - CI/CD pipelines or automated installations

output_examples.output:
Command: npm install -g git+https://github.com/JEO-tech-ai/supercode.git#main

This installs the package globally from the main branch. Verify with: supercode --version

Step 1: Generate SSH key with ssh-keygen -t ed25519
Step 2: Add public key to GitHub Settings > SSH Keys
Step 3: Install with npm install git+ssh://git@github.com:owner/private-repo.git

best_practices: Pin to specific version tags (#v1.0.0) instead of branch names for production stability
Use SSH authentication or environment variables for tokens - never hardcode credentials in package.json
Commit package-lock.json to ensure reproducible installations across environments
anti_patterns: Using #main or #master in production - branches change, causing unexpected updates
Hardcoding personal access tokens directly in configuration files
Using sudo to fix permission issues instead of configuring ~/.npm-global directory

faq.question: What is the difference between git+https and git+ssh URLs?
faq.answer: git+https uses HTTPS authentication (password or token), suitable for public repos. git+ssh uses SSH key authentication, required for private repos and more secure for automation.

faq.question: How do I install from a specific commit?
faq.answer: Append the commit SHA to the URL: npm install git+https://github.com/owner/repo.git#abc1234 where abc1234 is the full or shortened commit hash.

faq.question: Why am I getting permission denied errors?
faq.answer: Global npm installs require write access to system directories. Either use sudo, or configure a user directory with: npm config set prefix '~/.npm-global' and add it to your PATH.

faq.question: Can I install packages from private repositories?
faq.answer: Yes, using SSH keys or personal access tokens. SSH is recommended: set up an SSH key on GitHub, then use git+ssh://git@github.com:owner/repo.git format.

faq.question: What happens if the package has a prepare script?
faq.answer: npm automatically runs the prepare script after cloning. Ensure you have required build tools (like TypeScript) installed. Use --verbose flag to see build logs.

faq.question: How do I update a package installed from GitHub?
faq.answer: Unlike npm registry packages, git-installed packages do not update with npm update. Uninstall and reinstall: npm uninstall -g package && npm install -g git+https://...
